entry.title: How I Built an AI Agent That Generates SAP Analytical Artifacts (CDS VDM → Cube → Analytic Query)
entry.content:
After more than a decade running SAP projects out of Brazil, I can tell you the analytical chain in S/4HANA still eats more time than almost anything else my consultants do. Not the difficult part. The boring, mechanical part. Six tables, ten joins, four CDS layers, a Cube on top, then an Analytic Query, then a binding, then the View. Two weeks of work that nobody really enjoys.

A few weeks ago a customer dropped a 47-page functional spec on my desk for a Procurement KPI dashboard. I am not exaggerating about the page count. I read it twice and the deeper I got, the more obvious it became this was going to be the kind of project where the consultant builds for four weeks (or much more), the user looks at the result for two minutes, and then asks for a change.

So I tried something different. Instead of forwarding the spec to a developer, I dropped the PDF into the AI agent we have been building at Tachyonix and asked it to handle the artifact generation end to end. The first working version of the chain was in the system in under ten minutes (or less). The number sounds inflated when you write it down. It is the actual number, and that is exactly what I want to walk through in this post.

What we are building across the two posts

This is the first of two articles. The full pipeline takes a high-level functional requirement (something like a "Supplier 360 Portal") and produces the complete S/4HANA analytical stack: CDS VDM, then Cube, then Analytic Query, then Analytic View, and finally the dashboard binding. In this first post I cover the architecture and the foundation, which is the data model. Part 2 deals with the upper layers (Cube, Query, View) and the multi-chart dashboard.

I am keeping the screenshots and the actual customer data out of this on purpose. The point is the method, not a marketing demo.

How the pieces fit together

There is no real magic here. The interesting part is the division of labor between the LLM and the platform. The LLM is good at reading messy specs in Portuguese or English and turning them into structured intent. It is genuinely bad at producing valid CDS DDL on the first try, and even worse at remembering which release of S/4HANA the customer is on. So I do not let it write code. I let it produce metadata, and the platform translates that metadata into the actual SAP artifacts.

Layer

Component

What it actually does

Orchestration

AI Agent (Claude / Manus.ai)

Reads the spec, asks for clarification when needed, calls the Tachyonix OData API

Logic

Tachyonix Platform

Validates the JSON, maps fields, generates the SAP artifacts in the target package

Communication

OData APIs

Standard OData endpoints exposed by the Tachyonix engine

Execution

SAP S/4HANA

Where CDS Views, Cubes and Analytic Queries are actually activated against the chosen TR

In practice, the flow is quite linear:

  • I provide a PDF or a text spec. Sometimes I just paste a paragraph from an email.
  • The agent reads it and produces a JSON payload that describes the target artifact.
  • The agent posts that payload to Tachyonix via OData. The platform handles the SAP-side complexity.

Prerequisites

If you want to reproduce this in your own landscape, you need a few things in place. None of them are exotic. The agent is the only piece that is opinionated.

  • SAP S/4HANA, on-premise or Private Cloud. We have tested down to 1909 with some adjustments.
  • A Tachyonix instance, either deployed inside the same S/4HANA system as an add-on or reached over BTP through Cloud Connector.
  • An AI agent configured with a Claude-class model. We tried with smaller models and the quality of the JSON drops noticeably for complex joins.
  • An ABAP package and a Workbench transport already created. The agent does not create transports for you. That part is intentional.

Step 1. Letting the agent see the environment

Before generating anything, the agent has to know where it is going to write. I do not let it guess. The very first prompt of every session is a query about the environment, because if the agent gets the package or the TR wrong nothing else matters.

The prompt I always use:

"List the ABAP packages that contain the word 'DEMO' and show me my open Workbench and Customizing transport requests."

Expected output is plain text: a list of packages such as Z_TACHYONIX_DEMO or Y_DEMO_PROC, plus the user's open TRs. The agent then asks me to confirm which package and which TR to use. This single round-trip removes most of the hallucination problems we had in the early experiments, where the agent would invent a perfectly plausible package name that simply did not exist on the system.

Step 2. The data model (CDS VDM)

This is where it gets useful. I take a requirement like "purchasing analysis with material, plant and stock view, broken down by approved supplier and price agreement" and pass it on. In the customer case I mentioned at the top of this post, the input was much longer than that, but the structure of the prompt is always the same.

Here is the actual prompt template I use:

"Create an application on the Tachyonix platform. ABAP package: $PACKAGE. Request: $REQ. Application name: 'Supplier Portal 360'. Objective: consolidated view of approved suppliers, registration data, and active price agreements. Data sources: LFA1, EKKO, EKPO."

One thing I learned the hard way: the more context you put in the prompt, the better the output. "Approved suppliers" is not the same as "all suppliers", and the agent will not infer it from the table list. If your spec mentions approval flags, currency conversion, validity periods, or anything else that maps to a WHERE clause, you have to say it. Saying it once is enough. Saying it vaguely is not.

The agent then produces a payload. A trimmed example for this case looks like this:

{
  "appName": "Z_SUPPLIER_360",
  "package": "Z_TACHYONIX",
  "dataSource": [
    {"table": "LFA1", "alias": "Supplier"},
    {"table": "EKKO", "alias": "Header"},
    {"table": "EKPO", "alias": "Items"}
  ],
  "joins": [
    {"from": "Supplier.LIFNR", "to": "Header.LIFNR"}
  ]
}

The real payload that hits the platform is larger than this. It also carries field mappings, association resolution, currency and unit-of-measure references, and a few internal control fields. I have left those out for clarity. The platform fills in the missing pieces from its own metadata catalog before generating the actual CDS code.

Step 3. Validation and generation

Once the payload is ready, the agent does not just "write code". It posts the payload to the Tachyonix Builder, which validates the structure first, then generates and activates the CDS View directly in the target SAP system, against the TR I confirmed in Step 1.

The agent comes back with a direct URL to the generated artifact in the Builder, plus the activation status. If the activation failed (a missing field, a wrong cardinality, an authorization issue), the platform returns the SAP error message and the agent decides whether to retry, ask me a question, or stop.

The part that genuinely surprised me, because it was not something I had explicitly designed for, was how the agent handles the joins. I never had to write the SQL. It looked at the table list, recognized LFA1 and EKKO from the SAP data dictionary, and proposed the LIFNR link before I asked. For more complex chains, like joining EKPO back to MARA through MATNR, the same pattern held. It is not perfect (more on that in a second), but it removes a layer of mechanical typing that I never enjoyed anyway.

*** I will make a specific post commenting on the benefits of using Knowledge Graphs in conjunction with LLM and AI Agents.

What is next

Going from a blank screen to an active CDS VDM in the system took me roughly five minutes the last time I measured it carefully. No Eclipse session, no syntax errors, no half-finished WHERE clause to come back to in the morning. That is the foundation.

In Part 2 I will walk through the rest of the chain: how the same agent extends the VDM into a Cube, generates the Analytic Query, binds the Analytic View, and assembles a multi-chart dashboard. Without writing a single line of JavaScript, and without me touching the Tachyonix Builder UI directly. If you have specific tables in your landscape you want to see this run against, drop a comment, I am collecting examples for a follow-up post.

entry.title: Re: F110 Payment Work flow approval request not triggered...
entry.content:
In the standard workflow template WS23200018 (Payment Proposal Approval), the behavior you are seeing is expected. Once any agent approves, the workflow continues. The template is delivered with single‑step approval logic, not multi‑step parallel approvals. That is why your second approver never gets enforced.

If you require two mandatory approvals before payment can proceed, you need to adjust the workflow logic:

1) Workflow Step Customization
Copy WS23200018 into a custom workflow template. Replace the single approval step with either:

  • Two sequential approval steps (Agent 1 then Agent 2), or
  • Parallel approvals with “all must approve” condition.

2) Use Rule-Based Agent Assignment
Define separate rules for each approver (e.g., based on amount range). Ensure the workflow step requires both rules to be satisfied.

3) Custom Workflow Step / Enhancement
Implement a check in the workflow container. Only mark the step complete once both approvals are received. This can be done via workflow conditions or a small ABAP class method.

entry.title: Invalid namespace for Basic material
entry.content:
Hello,

we are experiencing an issue with Invalid namespace for Basic material:

In customizing activity 103908 Define Basic Materials we can not set up all necessary entries because of the limitation:


Message no. /CFG/CF_SSC_FRW031

Diagnosis

As per cloud framework guideline, a customer record cannot be created with SAP reserved pattern. This restriction is implemented to protect the customer data because if same records are delivered by SAP in futurereleases then customer created records will be overwritten.

Namespace restriction is applied on the key fields of an application.

Customers cannot create records starting with below mentioned SAP Best practice content namespace restrictions.

SAP reserved character fields - S, Q, R, X, P, Y

SAP reserved numeric fields - 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 0

Reference: 2326112 - Namespace restriction in SSCUI - SAP for Me

 

Does anybody else experience this issue? Have you found a suitable workaround?

 

Best regards,

Kerisha
